explanation:
C# runs on the .NET runtime (formerly .NET Core), a cross-platform, high-performance runtime. The syntax is similar to Java but with modern features: properties, delegates, LINQ, async/await, and pattern matching. The `dotnet` CLI handles project creation, building, testing, and publishing.

C# is strongly typed with type inference (`var`). Nullable reference types (C# 8+) catch null-related bugs at compile time. Records (C# 9+) provide immutable data containers with value equality. Top-level statements (C# 9+) eliminate boilerplate for simple programs.
key_takeaway: C# combines Java-like syntax with modern features — records for immutable data, LINQ for queries, nullable types for null safety, and pattern matching for expressive conditionals.
try_it: Create a `Product` record with Name, Price, and Category. Use LINQ to find the most expensive product per category and format the results as a table.

explanation:
C# supports full OOP with classes, interfaces, abstract classes, and generics. Properties replace Java's getter/setter methods with clean syntax. Events and delegates provide a built-in observer pattern. Sealed classes prevent inheritance; virtual/override enables polymorphism.

C# supports operator overloading, indexers, and extension methods — features that make APIs more expressive. The `interface` keyword defines contracts, and explicit interface implementation resolves naming conflicts.
key_takeaway: Properties, events, and extension methods make C# OOP more expressive than Java. Use `virtual`/`override` for polymorphism, `sealed` to prevent inheritance, and `abstract` for contracts.
try_it: Create a `Logger` class with events for `Info`, `Warning`, and `Error`. Subscribe multiple handlers (console, file) and demonstrate event invocation.

explanation:
LINQ (Language Integrated Query) lets you query collections with SQL-like syntax. Instead of nested loops and conditionals, you chain `Where`, `Select`, `OrderBy`, and `GroupBy`. Two syntaxes exist: method syntax (lambda-based) and query syntax (SQL-like). Both compile to the same code.

LINQ is lazy — it doesn't execute until you iterate (foreach, ToList, Count). Entity Framework translates LINQ to SQL for database queries. LINQ works on any `IEnumerable<T>`, making it the universal collection manipulation tool in C#.
key_takeaway: LINQ turns collection queries into readable chains — `Where` filters, `Select` transforms, `GroupBy` organizes. It's lazy until consumed and works the same on arrays, lists, and databases.
try_it: Use LINQ to find the youngest student per department with GPA above 3.0, then calculate the overall average GPA across all departments.

explanation:
C#'s async/await is the gold standard for asynchronous programming. `async` methods return `Task<T>` (or `Task` for void). `await` suspends the method until the task completes, freeing the thread to do other work. This makes async code read like synchronous code.

The key principle: don't block on async code. Never use `.Result` or `.Wait()` — they deadlock in UI and ASP.NET contexts. Use `ConfigureAwait(false)` in library code. `Task.WhenAll` runs multiple tasks concurrently; `Task.WhenAny` returns when the first completes.
key_takeaway: Use `async/await` for non-blocking I/O. Never `.Result` or `.Wait()` — they deadlock. Use `Task.WhenAll` for parallel execution and `CancellationToken` for timeouts.
try_it: Write an async method that fetches 5 URLs in parallel with a 3-second timeout, collects successful results, and reports which URLs failed.

explanation:
C# collections mirror Java's but with more LINQ integration. `List<T>` is the dynamic array. `Dictionary<TKey, TValue>` is the hash map. `HashSet<T>` stores unique elements. `Queue<T>` and `Stack<T>` are FIFO/LIFO. Immutable collections (`ImmutableList<T>`, `ImmutableDictionary<TKey, TValue>`) are thread-safe by design.

Generics in C# support constraints (`where T : class`, `where T : IComparable<T>`), covariance (`out`), and contravariance (`in`). Generic collections provide type safety without boxing/unboxing overhead.
key_takeaway: Use `List<T>` for dynamic arrays, `Dictionary<TKey, TValue>` for lookups, `ImmutableList<T>` for thread-safe collections. Generic constraints enforce type requirements at compile time.
try_it: Implement a `LRUCache<TKey, TValue>` class using `Dictionary` and `LinkedList` that maintains insertion order and evicts the oldest entry when capacity is reached.

explanation:
Delegates are type-safe function pointers — they define the signature of methods that can be passed as arguments. Events use delegates to implement the observer pattern: publishers raise events, subscribers handle them. This decouples components — the publisher doesn't know who's listening.

`Func<T, TResult>` and `Action<T>` are built-in delegate types that cover most use cases. Lambda expressions are the modern way to create delegate instances. Events use `event` keyword to restrict invocation to the declaring class.
key_takeaway: Delegates are type-safe function pointers. Events implement observer pattern with `event` keyword. Use `Func<>` for return values and `Action<>` for void operations.
try_it: Create a `StockPrice` class with a `PriceChanged` event. Subscribe a logger and an alert system that triggers when the price changes by more than 5%.

explanation:
ASP.NET Core is Microsoft's cross-platform web framework. It uses middleware pipelines — each middleware component processes the request and passes it to the next. Controllers define API endpoints with attributes (`[HttpGet]`, `[HttpPost]`). Dependency injection is built-in and used everywhere.

Minimal APIs (C# 10+) provide a lightweight alternative to controllers for simple endpoints. The framework includes built-in support for JSON serialization, model validation, authentication, and OpenAPI documentation.
key_takeaway: ASP.NET Core uses middleware pipelines and built-in DI. Minimal APIs define endpoints with `MapGet`/`MapPost`. Use records for DTOs and interfaces for testable services.
try_it: Add a `PUT /users/{id}` endpoint that validates the input, updates the user, and returns 200 OK or 404 Not Found. Add a middleware that rejects requests without an `X-API-Key` header.

explanation:
C# interview problems test your knowledge of LINQ, generics, async/await, and the .NET ecosystem. The most common patterns involve LINQ queries for data transformation, dictionary-based lookups, and string manipulation with StringBuilder. C#'s strong typing with generics makes it excellent for implementing data structures cleanly.

Beyond algorithms, C# interviews often cover dependency injection, async patterns (Task, async/await, cancellation tokens), and LINQ performance characteristics. Know when to use `IEnumerable<T>` vs `IQueryable<T>`, value types vs reference types, and the differences between `List<T>`, `Dictionary<TKey,TValue>`, and `HashSet<T>`. The .NET collections library is comprehensive — leverage it.
key_takeaway: Master Dictionary<K,V> for O(1) lookups and LINQ for elegant data transformations — they solve most C# interview problems.
try_it: Solve Two Sum using both a Dictionary and a LINQ-based approach. Compare readability and performance.

practice_challenges.title: Two Sum
practice_challenges.hint: Use a Dictionary<int, int> to store value-to-index. Check ContainsKey for the complement.
practice_challenges.description: Given an array of integers and a target, return indices of two numbers that add up to the target.

practice_challenges.title: Valid Parentheses
practice_challenges.hint: Use a Stack<char>. Push opening brackets, pop and compare on closing brackets using a Dictionary for mappings.
practice_challenges.description: Given a string of brackets, determine if the input is valid.

practice_challenges.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
practice_challenges.hint: Use a Dictionary<char, int> to track character positions. Slide left pointer when a duplicate is found within the window.
practice_challenges.description: Given a string, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.

practice_challenges.title: LRU Cache
practice_challenges.hint: Use a Dictionary<int, LinkedListNode> for O(1) lookup and a LinkedList for O(1) insertion/removal order.
practice_challenges.description: Design an LRU cache with O(1) get and put operations.
practice_challenges.example_input: LRUCache(2); put(1,1); put(2,2); get(1); put(3,3); get(2)

practice_challenges.title: Merge k Sorted Lists
practice_challenges.hint: Use a SortedSet or PriorityQueue<ListNode, int> (in .NET 6+). Add heads, poll smallest, push its next.
practice_challenges.description: Given k sorted linked lists, merge them into one sorted linked list.
